title: Editing Existing Code
subtitle: Modify your project with precision

sections.content:
Creating new files is impressive, but most real-world development is about **editing existing code**. Adding a feature to an app. Fixing a bug. Changing a design. Refactoring a function.

This is where Claude Code really shines. Because it can read your entire project, it understands the context. When you ask it to add a footer, it knows what HTML structure exists. When you ask it to change styling, it knows which CSS file to modify.

Claude doesn't start from scratch — it surgically edits exactly what needs to change, leaving everything else untouched.
sections.analogy: Imagine asking a contractor to add a room to your house. A bad contractor might knock down walls randomly. A good contractor studies the blueprints, understands the structure, and adds the room in exactly the right place. Claude Code is the good contractor.

sections.instruction: Ask Claude to add a footer to the HTML page you created in the last lesson. Be specific about what should be in it.
sections.prompt: Add a footer to index.html with copyright 2024 and a link to GitHub
sections.expectedResult: Claude edits the existing file, adding only the footer section. The rest of the page stays exactly as it was.

sections.instruction: Now ask Claude to modify the CSS. This is a targeted change — Claude should update only the relevant style rule.
sections.prompt: Make the nav bar sticky so it stays at the top when scrolling
sections.expectedResult: Claude modifies the CSS to add position: sticky and top: 0 to the nav bar. Only the nav styling changes — everything else remains the same.

sections.instruction: Now let's use a tool you learned in Level 4 — `git diff`. This shows you exactly what Claude changed. Green lines are additions, red lines are removals.

sections.content:
This is a critical habit: **always review changes before committing**. Claude is powerful but not perfect. You should always:

- Read through the diff to understand what changed
- Test the changes in your browser or by running the code
- Only commit when you're satisfied with the result

The `git diff` command is your best friend here. It shows you exactly what was added, removed, or modified — line by line.
keyPoints: git diff shows exactly what Claude modified
Green lines (+) = added code, red lines (-) = removed code
Claude edits surgically — it changes only what's needed

completionMessage: You've learned to use Claude Code for targeted edits to existing code. Now let's think bigger — building an entire project from a single description.
